title: Inflation dip may hold off rate rise
full_text:
Inflation has eased, offering hope the Reserve Bank might hold off on a further interest rate slug next month.
Annual headline inflation dipped to 4.2 per cent in the year to April, the ABS reported on Wednesday, a fall from the 4.6 per cent recorded through to March.
The trimmed mean measure, which strips out volatile items, was 3.4 per cent per cent, up from 3.3 per cent in the 12 months to March 2026.
The reading was broadly in line with market expectations and it means there is now less pressure on the central bank to hike rates again.
“We are firm on our view that the rate hike cycle is on hold,” Oxford Economics Australia economist Harry McAuley said in response to the numbers.
“Our outlook is contingent on a reopening of the Strait of Hormuz next month – the longer the conflict drags on, the higher the risk that inflation exceeds our expectations.
“Assuming tankers are able to exit the Strait sooner than later, we expect rates to remain at 4.35 per cent until late 2027.” In the year to March, headline inflation soared to 4.6 per cent, a sharp rise from the 3.7 per cent recorded in February.
In response, the RBA hiked rates for a third consecutive time to 4.35 per cent this month, citing fears of runaway inflation for the hike.
April’s ease in inflation follows a surprise jobs report last week that showed unemployment lifting to 4.5 per cent.
Deloitte Access Economics partner Stephen Smith said Wednesday’s inflation result was “unlikely to force” an RBA hike in June but would keep an August increase “firmly in play”. “The data suggests the effects of the conflict-driven global energy shock are starting to flow through the Australian economy, adding to price pressures and increasing the risk of another rate hike later this year,” he said.
